text: So laparoscopic enucleation was recommended and the patient was positioned in modified lithotomy position, a split leg with a surgeon between the patient's legs.
Normally, we proceed with a medial to lateral approach.

However, because it was questionable as to the laterality of the lesion, we started by mobilizing the descending colon and also mobilizing the splenic flexure in order to be able to anteriorly retract the spleen to gain access to the tail of the pancreas.

The gastrocolic ligament was eventually entered gaining access to the lesser sac and medial to lateral dissection was completed and you can see we're starting to enter the retroperitoneum gaining access to the pancreas.
Further dissection around the pancreatic tail was undertaken in order to be able to eventually try to lift the pancreatic tail here
And you can see the splenic vein.
And we're able to mobilize both the spleen and the pancreatic tail as a unit here.
Because the mass was not seen in the tail, medial dissection was taken to the superior mesenteric vein using the ultrasonic shears.

And here on posterior surface of the pancreas as it's lifted, there's a visual and palpable fullness there but it's not a discrete lesion that was readily identified so intraoperative ultrasound was subsequently performed.
and dissection was begun using cautery.
Unfortunately, due to the hypervascularity of these lesions, bleeding was encountered here.
